questions.question: Why is a queue not automatically a safe answer to bursty traffic?
options: Because backlog can grow beyond useful limits unless pressure is fed back to producers or low-value work is dropped.
Because queues cannot be used with retries of any kind.
Because consumers always process messages faster than producers create them.
Because queue depth alone guarantees freshness.
questions.explanation: A queue only moves pressure in time unless the rest of the system reacts to it.

questions.question: What is the goal of backpressure in an async system?
options: To make producers, brokers, and consumers coordinate around bounded work and useful freshness.
To keep brokers full so hardware stays utilized.
To make all workloads best-effort by default.
To remove the need for message prioritization.
questions.explanation: Backpressure is about bounded, coordinated throughput rather than silent backlog growth.

questions.question: Which signal is often more meaningful than queue depth alone?
options: Queue age, because it exposes whether messages are becoming stale or the pipeline is falling behind materially.
The number of database tables in the application schema.
Average pull request length in lines.
The total count of consumer instances ever launched.
questions.explanation: Queue age captures the freshness cost of delay, not just the amount of backlog.

questions.question: What is the main trade-off of rejecting or throttling producers when consumers lag?
options: You protect the shared pipeline, but some producers see explicit backoff or dropped work sooner.
You eliminate the need for all broker storage.
You guarantee no messages ever expire.
You make retry accounting unnecessary.
questions.explanation: Strong pressure propagation improves system health at the cost of earlier visible rejection.

questions.question: Why can retry workers be dangerous in an async pipeline?
options: They can quietly multiply load and backlog if they re-enqueue work faster than the system is recovering.
They always reduce latency because they keep workers busy.
They make queue age irrelevant.
They prevent any need for dead-letter handling.
questions.explanation: Async retries are still extra work, so they must respect the same pressure accounting.

questions.question: Which design best protects high-value work from a noisy low-value topic?
options: Separate classes, quotas, or isolated topics so one backlog does not starve the other.
A single shared queue with no message metadata.
Disabling expiration so old messages stay until eventually processed.
Removing consumer-level metrics to reduce noise.
questions.explanation: Isolation by class or topic is often necessary to preserve valuable traffic.

questions.question: Which metric best reveals that consumers are losing the race against producers?
options: Backlog growth delta where ingress persistently exceeds consume rate and queue age keeps rising.
The number of README files in the repository.
The count of canary releases in the past month.
Median CPU on an unrelated analytics cluster.
questions.explanation: Sustained positive backlog growth plus aging messages shows real pressure, not a brief burst.

questions.question: What weakens an interview answer about async backpressure most?
options: Treating the broker as infinite storage and never explaining who slows down or what work expires.
Distinguishing lossless from droppable traffic.
Using queue age to reason about freshness.
Explaining how producer throttling and consumer isolation fit together.
questions.explanation: The hard question is where pressure goes and how useful work is preserved.
